explanation: Procedure sets NOCOUNT OFF rather than ON, leaving row-count messages enabled and adding client/network noise.
Procedure uses transactions but sets XACT_ABORT OFF, allowing some runtime errors to leave partial work applied.
SELECT * couples callers to every current and future column of Sales.Orders and can break consumers when the table changes.
SELECT TOP (10) on Sales.Orders has no ORDER BY, so the engine returns an arbitrary set of rows.
DISTINCT is applied to o.OrderID, which is already unique as the primary key of Sales.Orders.
WITH (NOLOCK) on Sales.Orders can return uncommitted, missing, or duplicated rows.
YEAR(o.OrderDate) applies a function to the column and prevents a simple range seek on OrderDate.
Magic year value 2016 is embedded directly in the predicate instead of being parameterized.
int column o.CustomerID is compared to nvarchar variable @CustomerIdText, forcing an implicit conversion and harming index use.
OR-based optional filter (@CustomerID IS NULL OR o.CustomerID = @CustomerID) makes cardinality estimation and seeking difficult.
LIKE N'%urgent%' starts with a wildcard, preventing a normal index seek on Comments.
NOT IN (SELECT i.CustomerID ...) yields unexpected results if the subquery produces any NULL.
ISNULL(o.CustomerID, 0) wraps the column in a predicate and makes the search non-sargable.
Arithmetic on o.OrderID (OrderID + 0) prevents direct index matching on the key.
Inequality o.CustomerID <> 1 often scans broad portions of the table instead of seeking.
Business identifier 1 is hard-coded in the CustomerID predicate.
CROSS JOIN between Sales.Orders and Sales.Customers has no join predicate and multiplies the rowsets.
SELECT TOP (1) without ORDER BY returns an arbitrary Orders/Customers pair.
Comma-separated FROM list with join predicate in WHERE obscures the join and risks accidental cartesian products.
SELECT TOP (1) without ORDER BY returns an arbitrary matching OrderID.
Correlated scalar subquery counts OrderLines for every outer Orders row instead of using a set-based join/aggregate.
Correlated EXISTS on Sales.OrderLines can be expressed as a set join and may be evaluated per outer row.
The same CASE WHEN o.Comments IS NULL THEN 0 ELSE 1 END expression is repeated in the select list and ORDER BY.
ORDER BY 2 refers to a select-list position and becomes fragile when the select list changes.
OFFSET/FETCH ordered only by o.OrderDate has no stable unique tie-breaker, so paging can skip or repeat rows.
UNION performs duplicate removal between branches already disjoint by OrderID predicates; UNION ALL is sufficient.
Hard-coded business identifier OrderID = 1 is embedded in the first UNION branch.
Hard-coded business identifier OrderID = 2 is embedded in the second UNION branch.
COUNT(*) > 0 counts all matching OrderLines when only existence is required; EXISTS would be cheaper.
Correlated scalar COUNT(*) subquery runs per outer Orders row to test existence.
SELECT @OrderID = o.OrderID silently accepts multiple rows and keeps only the last assigned value.
Table variable @OrderIds may receive poor cardinality estimates, especially on older compatibility levels.
SELECT TOP (5) into @OrderIds has no ORDER BY, so which five OrderIDs are loaded is undefined.
SELECT ... INTO #ImplicitOrders creates the temp table schema implicitly instead of declaring it explicitly.
SELECT TOP (5) INTO #ImplicitOrders has no ORDER BY, so the loaded rows are arbitrary.
Cursor order_cursor processes rows iteratively for work that can be expressed set-wise.
Cursor SELECT TOP (2) has no ORDER BY, so which rows are fetched is undefined.
WHILE loop increments @Counter iteratively instead of using a set-based operation.
Dynamic SQL is built by concatenating CONVERT(nvarchar(20), @CustomerID) into the statement text.
User-supplied @CustomerID is concatenated directly into executable SQL before EXEC.
EXEC (@Sql) runs dynamic SQL without parameterized inputs, relying on concatenated values.
Dynamic SQL concatenates CONVERT(nvarchar(20), @OrderID) into the statement instead of binding a parameter.
sys.sp_executesql is invoked with a fully inlined statement and no parameter definitions/values.
Temporary table #Scratch is created explicitly and never dropped before the procedure ends.
CATCH block selects ERROR_MESSAGE() but neither rethrows with THROW nor returns a failure status.
RAISERROR is used for a new error path; THROW is preferred for modern error handling.
PRINT is used for diagnostics; it is buffered and unsuitable for structured logging.
Business identifier CustomerID = 1 is embedded directly instead of being passed as a parameter.
Unlabelled date constant '20160101' is embedded in the OrderDate filter.
CONVERT(date, '01/02/2016') omits an explicit style, so day/month order depends on session settings.
Unlabelled date string '01/02/2016' is embedded directly in conversion logic.
COALESCE on o.CustomerID and i.CustomerID in the join predicate inhibits normal join optimization and seeks.
SELECT TOP (1) without ORDER BY returns an arbitrary joined row.
Self-join of Sales.Orders on OrderID only retrieves the same key already available in the outer row.
SELECT TOP (1) without ORDER BY returns an arbitrary self-joined row.
ORDER BY o.Comments sorts on an unselective nvarchar(MAX) text/LOB column.
SUM(ol.Quantity) aggregates the entire Sales.OrderLines fact table without a restrictive filter.
Row-level predicate ol.OrderID > 0 is applied in HAVING instead of WHERE.
ORDER BY on COUNT(*) without GROUP BY is unnecessary because the aggregate returns exactly one row.
MERGE is used despite documented concurrency and correctness edge cases; separate INSERT/UPDATE is safer.
MERGE WHEN MATCHED sets target.Comments = target.Comments, writing no meaningful change while still locking and logging.
UPDATE sets Comments = Comments, producing no data change while still causing locking and logging.
DELETE FROM Sales.OrderLines uses broad non-key predicate Quantity = -1 rather than a stable key.
Magic value -1 is hard-coded in the DELETE predicate on Quantity.
WAITFOR DELAY intentionally blocks a worker thread instead of using asynchronous scheduling.
BEGIN TRANSACTION is started outside a TRY/CATCH error-handling boundary.
SET IDENTITY_INSERT ON changes session state and introduces fragile identity-management coupling.
EXEC sp_who invokes a system routine without a schema qualifier such as sys.
SET ROWCOUNT 1 changes a session setting and never restores it to 0 before the procedure ends.
INSERT INTO #DemoCategories VALUES (...) omits an explicit column list and will break if the table shape changes.
Magic category identifier 99 is hard-coded in the INSERT Values list.
LIKE '%' + @CustomerName + '%' uses a leading wildcard and prevents a normal seek on CustomerName.
Dynamic SQL concatenates @CustomerName into the LIKE pattern string.
User-supplied @CustomerName is concatenated directly into the dynamic SQL text before execution.
sp_executesql runs @sql without parameter definitions, leaving concatenated input unbound.
EXEC sp_executesql is called without a schema qualifier such as sys.sp_executesql.
UPDATE #DemoCustomers has no WHERE clause and will modify every row in the temp table.
Magic value CustomerCategoryID = 1 is hard-coded in the UPDATE assignment.
Predicate on outer-joined alias o (o.OrderDate >= ...) in WHERE rejects NULLs and effectively turns the LEFT JOIN into an INNER JOIN.
Unlabelled date constant '2016-01-01' is embedded in the OrderDate filter.
WHERE DeliveryMethodID = NULL uses equality to NULL; this never matches rows and should be IS NULL.
Scalar UDF dbo.GetCustomerType(CustomerID) in the SELECT list forces row-by-row evaluation.
CATCH block only selects ERROR_MESSAGE() and neither rethrows with THROW nor signals failure to the caller.
CATCH inside an active transaction does not ROLLBACK before handling the error, risking an open or doomed transaction.
Hard-coded index hint WITH (INDEX = 1) overrides the optimizer and is brittle in production code.
Query nests subqueries three or more levels deep and should be refactored to CTEs or temp tables.
LIKE '%A%' starts with a wildcard and prevents a normal index seek on CustomerName.
SELECT * from dbo.GetCustomerOrdersMulti returns every column and couples callers to the function's current shape.
dbo.GetCustomerOrdersMulti appears to be a multi-statement TVF; an inline TVF would typically optimize far better.
